actions.thought: 4-handed play with deep stacks (100BB). J8s is a standard UTG opening hand: suited connector with straight and flush potential, one broadway card. Folding is too tight, limping is weak and invites multiway pots. Raise to standard 3x BB size (300) to build pot, take initiative, and narrow field.

T5o (Ten-Five offsuit) is a weak, non-premium hand with poor playability post-flop. Elon raised from UTG (early position), which typically indicates a stronger range than a middle/late position raise. Being on the button is advantageous positionally, but this specific hand lacks the equity to justify a call here. The pot odds (1.5:1) would require roughly 40% equity, but T5o performs poorly against a UTG raise range and is particularly vulnerable to domination. Additionally, the small blind and big blind still have action, which could lead to further aggression and increased pot complexity. In the early stages of a 150-hand tournament, it's important to establish solid fundamentals by playing strong, premium hands rather than getting involved in marginal situations. This is a straightforward fold.

I have been dealt Pocket Aces, the best possible starting hand. Elon raised to 300 from UTG (effectively the Cutoff in this 4-max game). I need to 3-bet here for value and to protect my equity, especially being out of position. A standard 3-bet size from the Small Blind against a 3x open is around 3.5x to 4x. I will raise to 1,100 to build the pot and define my range.

Facing a re-raise to 1,100 after an UTG open, T3o is too weak to continue. The pot is 1,500 and calling 1,000 requires ~40% equity to break even — impossible for T3o multiway against an opener and a 3-bettor who both have much stronger ranges. 4-betting as a bluff is too risky with ~99BB stacks (can be 5-bet or called), and calling will put me in tough postflop spots dominated or with very low equity. Preserve stack early in the tournament and fold.

Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind after my UTG raise to 300. J8s is a marginal opening hand but speculative suited connector. SB 3-bet range is strong (QQ+, AKo/s, some bluffs possibly), and I'm OOP post-flop. Equity around 30-35% vs tight range, but poor playability and implied odds realization OOP deepstacked. Pot odds ~1.9:1 but fold equity lost by calling. 4-bet too strong for this hand. Standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament, no reads on opponents. Wait for better spots.

From UTG (earliest position pre-flop), I'm facing significant positional disadvantage throughout the hand. Qd7s is a weak, unmade hand that doesn't belong in my opening range from early position. The hand lacks both strength (no pair, weak kicker with Queen) and playability (offsuit, not connected). With deep stacks (100BB+), tight position, and 148 hands remaining in the tournament, folding is the correct play. I should preserve my chips and wait for premium hands in better positions. This is a standard, profitable fold.

I am on the button with 63o, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude has already folded. Although being on the button provides a strategic advantage, 63o is not a hand worth attempting to steal the blinds with in this position, especially in the early stages of a tournament. The risk-to-reward ratio for opening such a weak hand is unfavorable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have 9♦4♥ — a weak, unconnected offsuit hand and I’ll be out of position post‑flop. Folding is cautious but calling is very cheap: I only need to put in 50 more chips to see a flop, which gives me about 4:1 pot odds (pot becomes 200 for a 50 call). Raising OOP with this garbage is unnecessary and will often create difficult spots or get re‑raised. I’ll call to see a flop and then proceed only if I improve or get a favorable texture; I’ll fold to significant aggression if I miss.

9s5c is a very weak offsuit disconnected hand with poor playability and showdown value. Heads-up vs SB limper after folds, pot 200 with blinds posted. Checking takes a free flop cheaply. Postflop, SB acts first, allowing easy fold if I miss (high likelihood). Raising with this hand risks spew: minraise to 200 adds 100 but gets called by better hands frequently, leading to difficult OOP spots. Deep stacks early in tournament favor playing postflop cautiously rather than bluff-raising trash. Preserves stack for better spots over 150 hands.

I have 9♦4♥ — I completely missed this Q♣ T♣ T♠ board: no pair, no club draw. The flop is extremely hostile to a blind-calling range — Elon (BB) can easily have Tens, Queens, or combinations that make trips/full houses or at least a calling hand. With ~9.8k stacks and only 200 in the pot, a small C‑bet will get called by most of his continuing range and a raise would put me in a very bad spot. There is no fold equity and very little chance to improve profitably. Best line is to check and concede the initiative — give up to aggression and only continue with a clear improvement or a very cheap card. If he checks back I can reassess on the turn; if he bets, I will fold to meaningful pressure.

9s5c remains complete air on QcTcTs flop: no pair, no draws, dominated by any piece of the board. Heads-up OOP vs SB limper who checked flop (weak range likely). Pot 200, deep stacks (96bb). Betting risks bloating pot with 0 equity, easy call/raise from better hands/draws. Checking free cards turn or induces bluffs/value bets I can fold to profitably. Early tournament, no need for speculative bluffs; preserve stack for premium spots over 150 hands.

I hold 9♦4♥ — completely missed: no pair, no club, no backdoor. The pot is only 200 and it costs me nothing to check. Betting here as a bluff has poor risk/reward (I have no blockers to K/Q/T and a raise would put me in a very bad spot OOP). I’ll check to control the pot and fold to meaningful aggression; if checked through I’ll reassess on the river and only continue with a clear improvement or a very favorable river/card-based bluff opportunity.

9s5c is pure air on QcTcTs-Kh board: no pair, no draws, zero equity vs any reasonable range. Heads-up OOP vs SB limper who has checked three times (pre/flop/turn), indicating weakness but still ahead of my nothing. Pot 200, deep stacks (96bb effective). Betting bluffs here risks 100+ into 200 with no fold equity against checking range that caps low (pairs/draws that missed turn); easy calls/shoves from better. Checking is mandatory: takes free river card, induces SB bluffs/value thin bets I can fold to cheaply (preserving stack), or free showdown if checked through. Early in 150-hand tournament, no need for hero bluffs with trash; survival and premium spot waiting optimal.

I hold 9♦4♥ and completely missed this river: board Q♣ T♣ T♠ K♥ A♣. The board contains trips (tens) plus three clubs, so opponent continuing ranges include trips/full houses, club flushes and even straights (e.g. J9). I have no pair, no club and no meaningful blockers, so bluffing here has very poor fold equity and great downside given deep stacks vs a tiny pot (200). Both players checked the turn, suggesting showdown or strong but non‑aggressive holdings; betting would often get called by any pair/club. Best, lowest‑risk play is to check and take showdown (and fold to a bet). Action: check.

9s5c is pure bluff-catcher air on this QcTcTs-Kh-Ac board: no pair, straight (e.g., J9 best possible but we don't have it), flush, or improved draws. Heads-up vs SB limper who checked four streets (pre/flop/turn/river), capping their range weak (missed draws, low pairs like 99-JJ, uncoordinated broadways that didn't connect). Pot 200 (1BB), deep effective stacks (~96BB). Betting any size (min 100, pot ~600) offers poor risk/reward: SB folds <50% (calls thin value/low pairs, shoves better), no fold equity vs capped checking range, commits chips early in 150-hand tournament unnecessarily. Checking optimal: induces SB bluffs/value thin bets (fold profitably), free showdown (unlikely win but 0 cost), preserves stack for premium spots. Survival > spew in deep tournament.

I am dealt 8h 2d in the UTG position. This is one of the weakest possible hands in poker, lacking both connectivity and high-card value. In a 4-max game, standard opening ranges are wider, but this hand still falls well outside the range of hands that can be profitably played from any position, especially UTG. There is no reason to put chips in the pot with this hand.

I’m on the button with A♥7♠ — a playable, positional hand with blocker value and a backdoor spade. With UTG folded this is a standard open-versus-blinds spot: I want fold equity and to play postflop in position. I’ll raise to 300 (3×BB) to pressure the blinds, deny good odds to limp/call, and keep the pot manageable with ~99bb effective stacks. If I face a small 3-bet I’ll consider calling to realize position; vs a large 3-bet I’ll fold. Postflop I’ll c-bet when I connect or on favorable textures and use position to control pot size and extract value when I pair my Ace or hit draws.

QsJc is a strong offsuit broadway hand with good equity against a button raising range, which is likely wide in this stealing spot early in the tournament with deep stacks. Facing a 3bb raise from the button, calling from the SB provides excellent pot odds (250 to call into 450, about 35% equity needed, and we have ~40%+ vs. their range heads-up). Postflop playability is decent, and while OOP is a disadvantage, the implied odds and potential multiway pot with BB make it profitable long-term. Folding is too tight, and 3-betting is unnecessary with this hand—we can realize equity postflop. BB acts last, but their squeeze range is narrow enough that this spot is +EV.

I'm in the big blind with 4♣J♦, a weak off-suit hand that plays poorly from out of position. While the cost to call is minimal at 200 chips, there are already two committed opponents (Minni raised from the button to 300, and Elon called from the small blind). 4-J lacks the strength to profitably play in a multi-way pot, especially when both opponents will have positional advantage post-flop. In a high roller tournament, I need to be disciplined about hand selection. Folding weak hands preserves my 9,900 chip stack for premium opportunities where I have better equity. This is a clear fold situation.

Preflop, we called a wide button raise OOP with QJo for its equity and playability. The flop 5c5d9s is a dry, paired board that misses us completely—no pair, no draws, just queen-high. OOP first to act into a 700 pot against a BTN who raised wide preflop. Our range here includes some 5x, 98s, A9/A5 etc., so we can rep some value, but betting OOP risks getting raised/folded out or called by better. Checking allows us to realize some showdown value if villain checks behind (BTN checks a lot here), control pot, and pot control with air. If villain bets small, we have fold equity against their floats/bluffs; larger bets we fold. Donk betting is -EV OOP with nothing early in tournament. Check is standard to pot control and induce bluffs.

I opened preflop on the button with A♥7♠ to take initiative and play post‑flop in position. The flop (5♣‑5♦‑9♠) is very dry and I missed, but I have an Ace blocker and a backdoor spade. Elon (SB) checked, and his calling range vs my open is wide and contains many hands that missed this flop. A half‑pot continuation bet applies pressure, picks up the pot often vs overcards and floats, and keeps the pot manageable if I run into a made 5 or 9. I’ll size down to ~50% pot to get folds while retaining maneuverability on the turn — if called I’ll proceed cautiously and re-evaluate on the turn (attack aces/7s or check/control vs signs of strength).

Facing a 1/3 pot c-bet on a dry, paired 5c5d9s flop with complete air (queen-high, no pair or draw). Preflop, we defended wide OOP from the SB against a button steal-raise, but this board misses our range entirely. Villain's BTN range is wide and continues frequently with value (sets, two-pair like 95/54, overpairs TT+/AJ+), draws (clubs, gutshots), and bluffs/floats. Calling commits 350 into a growing pot OOP with no equity realization plan—turn playability is poor as we'll likely check-fold again or get blown out. Pot odds require ~25% equity, but we have far less against their polarized betting range (~10-15% at best, mostly live with bluffs but capped). Raising as a bluff is -EV OOP: villain has strong value to continue and our check doesn't rep monsters well enough to fold out better. Folding preserves our deep stack (94bb) early in the tournament for better spots. Consistent with preflop plan to pot control and fold to aggression without improvement.

UTG with 9♠2♠ is a clear fold. This hand has very poor equity and plays horribly out of position against the remaining players. Limping 100 invites raises from the button or blinds and puts me in tough spots; raising is also a mistake because I'll be dominated or face a 3-bet. With ~103BB effective and 146 hands remaining, preserving my stack and waiting for a better spot is optimal.

Preflop on the button with J♥T♦ offsuit after UTG fold. Excellent position heads-up vs blinds with deep stacks (~94-103BB effective). JTo is a strong open-raising hand from the button due to playability, straight potential, and decent equity against calling ranges. Pot is 150 (SB+BB posted), to call 100. Raising steals blinds often and builds pot in position. Standard sizing: 3x BB to 300 total (2.5x effective after blinds). Min-raise too small, invites multiway. Will fold to 3bet if overly strong, but proceed cautiously postflop with position.

5-Jack offsuit is a weak holding that doesn't warrant investment from the small blind. Elon's button raise to 300 is standard and represents a wide range. More importantly, the big blind (Flash) still has to act, and I would be playing out of position postflop if I call. The risk of facing a 3-way pot with a marginal hand is unfavorable. In a tournament setting early on, preserving chips for premium spots is strategically sound. Folding here is the profitable decision as it avoids putting chips at risk with inadequate hand strength.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-4 offsuit. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. While his range is likely wide, 7-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Even with decent pot odds, the lack of playability and high probability of being dominated or having to fold to further aggression makes this a clear fold. I will preserve my stack for more favorable situations.

UTG with Ts5h offsuit is a complete trash hand, one of the worst starting hands in poker. No suitedness, connectivity, or high card strength. Folding pre-flop from early position preserves my deep stack (95BB) for better spots. No information on opponents' tendencies yet to justify a limp or raise, and limping is suboptimal. Standard tight-aggressive play dictates an immediate fold.

I'm on the button with 7-2o (seven-deuce), which is the worst starting hand in poker. Despite the positional advantage and only needing to call 100 chips, 7-2o has extremely poor equity and should be folded in virtually all situations. The hand is dominated and rarely makes a profitable equity play. While position is valuable, it doesn't overcome the fundamental weakness of this particular hand. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities on the remaining 145 hands.

32 offsuit is the weakest starting hand in poker. Even in a blind-vs-blind situation, it has extremely poor playability and I would be out of position post-flop. There is no reason to invest more chips into the pot with such a weak hand.

With T2o (Ten-Deuce offsuit) from UTG, this is a clear fold. T2o is one of the weakest starting hands in poker - it has minimal equity against most ranges and is easily dominated. From early position, I have no information about opponent action and should be playing only premium hands (premium pairs, AK, AQ, etc.). Even in a tournament setting where chip accumulation matters, playing trash hands from the worst position is a fast way to lose value. The standard professional play here is to fold and wait for better spots.

I am on the button with J5o. This hand is significantly below the standard opening range for a 4-handed game, even from late position. Given the stack depths and the fact that I want to maintain a strong table image, I will fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity.

I’m in the small blind with 3h7h — a weak but suited hand. It’s heads-up vs the big blind and the cost to see a flop is only 50 more (I’ve already posted 50), so calling is a cheap way to realize equity and avoid playing a dominated raising range out of position. Raising to 200 would overcommit a poor hand and invite a 3-bet or tough postflop spots. Plan: call to see the flop; proceed only with meaningful equity (pair, good heart draw, or strong redraws) and otherwise fold to heavy pressure to keep the pot controlled.

Pre-flop in the big blind facing a small blind limp from Minni. My holding is 8h9d, offsuit connectors with some playability but not strong enough to raise profitably out of position with deep stacks. SB limps are often weak or speculative hands. Checking realizes equity on favorable flops at no cost, while a raise risks being called by better hands or dominated and playing poorly OOP post-flop. Preference is to see a flop heads-up cheaply and control the pot.

Flop: 5c 4s 4h; my hand 7h3h. I have no pair — only 4 outs (a 6) to make a straight (~16% to hit by the river) and a very remote runner-runner heart flush. I’m out of position and the pot is still small (200). Leading as a bluff here is high-risk: many hands that beat me (any 4, any 5, small pairs, overcards with backdoor equity) will either call or raise, and I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with very limited equity. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize my equity for free, and keeps options open. Plan: check now; if BB checks back I see a free turn; if he makes a small probe bet I can call to try to hit my outs, but I will fold to a large bet since continuing will be costly with marginal equity. If I improve (make a 6 or pair) I’ll re-evaluate for value.

Flop of 5c-4s-4h completely misses my 8h9d holding—no pair, no straight draw (gutshot unlikely), no flush draw. SB limp range includes many hands that connect with this low paired board: pocket 4s (set), 45, 34, A4/5, low pairs like 22-33, or draws. Overcards 89 are dominated and lack showdown value. OOP as BB, first to act after villain's check—betting as a bluff is thin since villain can have strong hands to check-raise or float; thin value unlikely. Best to check for pot control, keep pot small, and realize any thin equity on later streets cheaply. Folding to bets remains standard post-check.

Check. With 7h3h on 5c-4s-4h-Td I have no pair, no realistic flush draw (only one heart on board) and only four outs (the 6s) to make a straight on the river (~4/46 ≈ 8.7% equity). I’m out of position and the pot is small (200). Betting as a bluff here is high-risk and will often get called or raised by worse or better hands; checking controls the pot and lets me realize my tiny equity for free. Plan: check to the river and fold to any meaningful turn bet; if Elon checks back I’ll see the river and only continue if I hit.

Turn board brings the Td, pairing nothing for me and adding an overcard that hits villain's SB limp range (e.g., T9, AT suited, JT) or straight draws like 67, but more importantly, strengthens check-back ranges like Ax, KQ, suited connectors that missed flop. My 89dd remains a complete air hand: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot at best to J for unlikely straight), no flush draw. Villain's double check suggests weakness—no strong made hands like sets or two-pair would check twice OOP—but they can still call a bet with middle pairs, overs, or floats. Betting as a bluff risks being called by better showdown hands while building a pot OOP with no equity. Checking realizes zero equity cost-free, induces villain bluffs on river, and keeps pot small for hopeless hand. Pot control and cheap fold to river aggression remains optimal.

I missed my equity. With 7h3h on 5c-4s-4h-Td-Ks I have no pair and no realistic draw on the river. The pot is tiny (200) versus deep stacks (~10k), and any river bluff here will be called by a wide range of hands that beat me (any 4, 5, T, K, or a small pair). I controlled the pot by checking previous streets and planned to fold to meaningful pressure; since I didn’t improve I should not bluff. Action: check and fold to a bet.

River check from Minni continues to represent weakness after limping SB and checking three streets on a paired 4s board with scattered overcards (Td, Ks). My 8h9d is pure air—no pair, no completed draw (gutshot to J was live on flop but missed), no showdown value against villain's range which includes pairs (44 full house potential, low pairs), Ax/Kx overs that checked down, or missed draws. Betting as bluff is -EV: villain's check-call range on prior streets favors calling stations with medium strength that beat my bluff; pot is small (200), and position is poor (acting second but OOP generally). Checking induces villain bluffs (e.g., missed draws like 23, 36, clubs), gets to showdown cheaply if villain has show-down value worse than nothing? No, but primarily pot control and zero cost to fold to nothing. Optimal passive line preserves stack for better spots.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with 3-5 offsuit. This hand is significantly below the opening range for this position and has very poor playability post-flop. I will fold and wait for a more favorable hand.

Button with Td8d (suited one-gapper) — a very playable, positionally advantaged hand. Stacks are deep (~100bb), pot is only 150 and I can steal two blinds or play postflop in position. Elon (SB) has shown frequent preflop aggression but Claude (BB) has folded a lot; that makes a raise profitable. I want meaningful fold equity while keeping the pot manageable, so open to 3x (300). Plan: if both fold I take the pot; if called I'll use position to c-bet favorable flops and play cautiously on heavy action. If Elon 3-bets large I will fold most of the time (only continue vs small 3-bets or with a strong read). Raise to 300 to apply pressure and leverage positional advantage.

73 suited is a marginal hand at best, gapped and low cards make it unsuitable for calling a button raise out of position in the small blind. Pot odds are about 450:250 (1.8:1 effective), but post-flop play OOP against an aggressive range from Minni is disastrous. No reason to raise with trash. Folding preserves our deep stack for better opportunities in this early tournament stage.

I'm in the Big Blind with 8c2d, one of the worst possible starting hands in poker. Minni raised to 300 from the button, and Elon folded. While the pot odds are decent (2.25:1 to call 200), 8-2 offsuit has extremely limited equity and is very difficult to play post-flop. Against a standard opening raise, this hand has minimal value. With 7 hands in and 143 remaining, it's essential to preserve my stack and wait for premium opportunities. A fold here is the correct play.

UTG with 4♦9♣ – a weak, unsuited, poorly connected hand with terrible post‑flop playability and a high chance of being dominated. Calling 100 into a 150 pot out of position with three players left invites multiway action and potential raises; raising myself to 200 is equally unjustified. I preserve my deep stack (10400) for better opening spots and fold now.

Folded to me on the button with QJo offsuit, a strong hand in this spot. Deep stacks, excellent position postflop. Standard play is to open-raise 3x the big blind to 300 to build the pot, take initiative, and isolate the blinds who will often fold or call out of position with worse hands. QJo has good equity and playability.

Facing Elon's button raise to 300 from the small blind with 9s5s. This is a very weak hand with only modest equity against a typical button opening range. While the pot odds are approximately 1.8:1 (250 to call, 450 in pot), 9s5s lacks the equity needed to profitably call out of position preflop in a 4-handed game.

Key considerations:
- Hand strength: 9s5s has roughly 25-30% equity vs Elon's likely raising range
- Position: Out of position (SB) facing aggression
- Tournament stage: Hand 8/150 - early enough to be selective and fold weak hands
- Flash (BB) still needs to act, which complicates our decision further
- Better spots will come

This is a clear fold. We should preserve chips and play disciplined poker, waiting for premium hands or better situations to engage in pots.

I am in the Big Blind holding 4s 2s. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. While I have 3.25 to 1 pot odds to call, 42s is a very weak hand to play out of position. Even though Elon likely has a wide opening range from the Button, defending with such a low-equity hand often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I am forced to fold or bluff with limited equity. I will fold and wait for a better spot to contest a pot.

Pocket Queens is a premium hand UTG in 4-handed play. Raising is standard to build the pot, isolate, and thin the field. Previous raises to 300 have been effective. Size to 3x the BB (300 total) balances value and fold equity against loose callers seen in history.

I'm on the button with 4d-Td against Elon's 300 raise. While this isn't a premium hand, several factors favor a call:

1. **Position Advantage**: Button is the strongest position; I act last post-flop and can make informed decisions based on opponent actions.

2. **Reasonable Pot Odds**: The pot will be 450 after Elon's raise, and I'm calling 300. Pot odds are 1.5:1 (need ~33% equity), which is workable.

3. **Hand Potential**: 4d-Td has decent potential—two diamonds for a flush draw, and the 4 could hit the wheel if we get to the turn/river.

4. **Elon's Sizing**: His 3BB raise is standard and doesn't indicate exceptional strength. Based on previous hands, he folds to pressure when outdrawn.

5. **Stack Depth**: I have 9700 chips, sufficient to navigate post-flop dynamics with fold equity if needed.

6. **Expected Blind Folding**: The blinds will likely fold out, leaving me in a potentially favorable heads-up spot where my position becomes even more valuable.

Playing in position against a single raiser with a reasonable stack is standard poker strategy. I'll evaluate the flop carefully and leverage my button advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Trey offsuit (Jh 3s), which is a very weak hand. Elon opened from UTG and Claude called on the Button. J3o performs poorly multi-way and I would be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Given the strength shown by the open and the call, there is no justification for continuing with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Elon opened to 300 UTG and the Button called, SB folded. I’m in the BB with Qh9c; pot is 750 and I must call 200 more (300 total). Q9o is a marginal but defensible hand from the big blind in a 4-max game — pot odds (~200 into a 750 pot ≈ 21%) make seeing a flop cheap and stacks are deep (~100bb) so there’s postflop maneuverability. I don’t want to 3-bet light here: Q9o has poor playability OOP against an UTG opener and a caller and is vulnerable to 4-bets/isolation. Folding would be too tight given the price. Plan: call to see a flop, play cautiously out of position, continue only with top pair/strong draws or good turn improvements, and fold to heavy aggression without improvement.

Board: A♣ T♠ K♥. I hold Q♥9♣ — I completely missed. My only realistic equity is 4 jacks to make the Broadway straight on the turn and 3 queens to pair (which is a weak pair on this texture). Elon opened UTG so his range is heavy in A/K/broadways; the Button caller can have similar or better holdings. Out of position against two opponents I won’t build the pot with a marginal hand or bluff that has poor blockers. I check to control the pot, see a free turn, and will fold to any meaningful bet unless I pick up a clear improvement (J/Q) or a very cheap spot to continue.

Preflop UTG raise with QQ is standard premium hand. Flop Ac Ts Kh is scary with A and K high cards, but as PFR facing BB check, a continuation bet maintains aggression, denies equity to straight draws (e.g., QJ, JT), floating ranges, and weaker Ax/Kx that may fold. History shows opponents passive postflop (frequent check/calls but no aggression). 3-way pot but Minni weak check first in. Bet 50% pot for value from weaker pairs/draws and fold equity. If raised or called multiway, QQ likely beat but can fold turn. Deep stacks allow flexibility.

Calling Elon's 500 flop raise on the button with 4d-Td. I have bottom pair with a strong flush draw (14 outs) plus backdoor possibilities. Pot odds of 3.9:1 require ~20% equity, and my hand has ~25-30% against his likely range (AK, AQ, KQ, or continuation bet). Position advantage is crucial—I act last on remaining streets and can leverage this if another diamond or under-card falls. Stack depth (8900 remaining) is comfortable for making disciplined decisions on the turn. Elon shows weakness by checking post-flop in previous hands when facing aggression.

Fold. I have Q♥9♣ on A♣ T♠ K♥ — I completely missed. Elon (UTG) bets 500 and Claude calls, putting the pot at 1,950; my call is 500 into a 2,450 pot, so immediate pot odds ≈ 20.4%. My realistic clean outs are basically the 4 jacks to make Broadway (plus 3 queens that would still be a very weak pair and unlikely to win). Chance to make the straight by the river ≈ 16.4% (1 - (43/47)*(42/46)); adding the weak pair/rare backdoor flush does not get me to break-even. Also I’m out of position against an opener + caller with ranges heavy in A/K/T/broadways, so I’m dominated and raising as a bluff OOP into two players has poor fold equity and bad blockers. With ~101bb effective, preserve chips for better spots — fold.

The board is Ac Ts Kh Ks, a paired kings turn after a flop c-bet was called by Claude from the button. QQ is a strong preflop hand but vulnerable on this high-card heavy board with straight and flush draw potential pre-turn. Claude's flop call suggests a range including Ax, Kx, QJ/JT for straights/draws, or sets. The Ks pairs the king, improving Kx hands to trips or two pair (e.g., KQ now has kings and tens), putting QQ likely behind much of his calling range. Pot is 1950, stacks deep (~90BB). Checking achieves pot control, realizes equity vs worse (AJ/AQ), induces bluffs or thin value bets from Ax/draws that missed, and avoids stacking off to Kx. History shows passive opponents (check/call heavy), so he'll often check back Ax. If he bets big, we can fold profitably; small bets callable. No need to bet thin as value range is narrow and he can easily have better overpairs or improved hands. Checking is GTO-correct balancing aggression with protection.

Checking the turn after Elon's check. The Kings on turn likely improved his holdings (if he has AK or any King). My pair of tens is marginal and I'm likely trailing. However, I have 10 outs to a flush draw (~20-21% equity). By checking, I get a free river card—if a diamond comes, I win; if not, I lose a smaller pot than if I bet and faced a raise. This is the mathematically sound play: extracting value from my draw equity while minimizing risk when behind.

River 2s is a low blank that doesn't improve QQ and may complete spade flushes (board has Ts, Ks, 2s). Heads-up vs Claude's button calling range after flop c-bet, which includes strong Ax/Kx, broadway draws that may have hit, or monsters; our QQ blocks some straights but is crushed by top pair/top kicker or better. Turn check-check pot-controlled, maintaining thin value from weaker Ax/pairs while avoiding bloating pot OOP. Betting river risks getting called by better (AK/AQ/KQ/trips) with minimal fold equity against passive history (check/call tendencies), and thin value from AJ/JJ unlikely given board texture. Checking maximizes EV: induces check-behinds from worse (AJ/TT/JJ/draws), allows calling value bluffs or thin bets, and pot control preserves stack for better spots in tournament. GTO-solver prefers check-fold or check-call balancing here OOP with vulnerable overpair.

River play with 4d-Td on board Ac-Ts-Kh-Ks-2s. I made a pair of tens, which is a marginal but showdown-worthy hand. Elon checked both the turn and river, indicating weakness or a hand not confident enough to bet. My diamond draw missed, so my ten pair is my final equity. Key considerations: (1) Elon's check-down line suggests he either has a weak Ace, a marginal King, or is completely bluffing. (2) If I bet, strong Kx hands raise me, forcing a fold on a marginal hand. If he's bluffing, he folds anyway without giving value. (3) With a pair of tens showing relative strength on this board, checking to showdown is optimal. My stack of 8900 is healthy, and winning a 1950 chip pot without further risk is sound. (4) Elon's passive line through two streets after his flop aggression indicates he's not confident in his holdings. I take the free showdown with my made hand.

I'm in UTG with QK, one of the premium starting hands in poker. This hand warrants aggressive play despite my early position. With a healthy 8,900 chip stack and opponents well-covered, I want to:

1. Establish hand strength and take down the pot quickly
2. Avoid multiway pots where QK loses its edge
3. Apply pressure at a table that's shown consistent aggression (raises to 300+ in multiple hands)

A raise to 350 (3.5x the big blind) is standard for a premium hand in early position. It's strong enough to assert dominance without overcommitting to the hand preflop. This sizing should fold weak holdings while potentially allowing me to build a pot if called by stronger opponents where I have decent equity.

Given the tournament stage (hand 10/150) and stack depth, I should be playing aggressively with premium hands to build my stack early.

J7o is a weak hand to play against an early position raise from Claude. Although I am on the button, the hand strength does not justify a call or a 3-bet bluff against an UTG range in a 4-handed game. Folding is the standard and most profitable long-term play.

Facing an UTG open to 3.5bb in a 4-handed game, A♣5♦ from the small blind is a marginal, often-dominated hand. Calling 300 into a 500 pot gives only ~37.5% break-even equity; out of position vs the raiser (and still facing the big blind) I’ll frequently be playing second-best or in difficult multi-way spots. A thin 3-bet here is light and likely to face a call or a 4-bet, which I don’t want to navigate this early with a weak kicker. With ~100bb effective stacks and plenty of hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better, positionally-favorable spot is the right long-term play. Therefore I fold.

Pocket 66 is a strong hand heads-up against a UTG raiser after folds. Deep stacks, I'm OOP in BB, so 3-betting is better than calling for value, protection, and fold equity. Claude has shown a relatively tight range from history. Sizing to ~3x the raise (1050 total) builds the pot while maintaining balance.

I'm facing a 3x re-raise from Elon to 1,050 with QK (Qh-Kc), one of the premium starting hands. This situation warrants a decisive response.

Key factors:
1. **Hand strength**: QK is a strong unpaired hand with excellent equity against most likely holdings (37% vs AA, 47% vs AK, 55% vs AQ, much better against non-premium hands)
2. **Stack depth**: I have 8,550 chips. A re-raise to 2,400 total commits 2,050 more, leaving me 6,150 in reserve - a healthy stack that allows me to play the flop confidently
3. **Positional advantage**: After Elon's re-raise, I have the information advantage and can apply significant counter-pressure
4. **Table dynamics**: Elon has shown aggression throughout the session, but also folds to pressure (see Hand #1)
5. **Tournament stage**: Hand 10 of 150 - I should be building my stack with premium hands in aggressive situations

A re-raise to 2,400 total:
- Punishes his aggression and forces him to make a difficult decision
- Either wins the pot immediately or builds it with a hand I can comfortably play
- Applies pressure that's proportional to my hand strength
- Avoids allowing him to outmaneuver me with aggression alone

This is a strong re-raise that maintains my stack health while taking control of the hand.

Pocket 66 was a standard 3-bet preflop over Claude's UTG open, but his 4-bet to 2400 indicates a very strong premium range (QQ+, AKo, maybe JJ). From hand history, Claude plays tightly and passively, folding frequently early on and rarely showing aggression, making this 4-bet highly selective. I'm OOP heads-up with effective stacks of ~6500 (his stack), and 66 has poor equity (~20-25%) against his range and plays poorly postflop. Pot odds are ~2.6:1 but not enough given the reverse implied odds and position disadvantage. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this early stage tournament (hand 10/150).

I am in the UTG position (4-handed) with Queen-Eight offsuit. While 4-handed play allows for a wider opening range than a full ring, Q8o is still a marginal hand that plays poorly post-flop and is easily dominated by better Queens or standard calling ranges. Given that Claude and Elon have shown recent aggression, I will fold this hand and wait for a better spot to maintain my stack.

On the button with 6♠4♣ (a weak, marginal hand) but facing only the blinds and with deep stacks, I want to use position and fold equity to try to steal the pot. Limping/calling 100 keeps both blinds in and gives me a multiway pot with a poor hand; a small open-raise is standard. I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3x the BB) — a size that applies pressure, discourages easy calls, and is consistent with the table’s prior sizing. Plan: if both fold I take the pot; if one calls I’ll play postflop with position (c-bet good texture-dependent, pot-control on dangerous boards); if I face a sizable 3-bet (large and indicative of strength) I will fold this hand rather than get involved with marginal equity.

Facing a min-raise to 300 from the button (Minni, who has shown aggression in prior hands). My K5s is suited with a king blocker, offering good playability for flush draws and potential straight draws postflop. Pot odds are attractive: calling an additional 250 into a 450 pot (~36% of pot). SB defense vs BTN wide range realizes equity over multiple streets, especially deep-stacked (~100BB). BB (Claude) likely to check behind frequently. Folding a speculative suited king OOP vs minraise is too tight; prefer calling to control pot size and exploit Minni's possible wide range.

Calling with As9h from the big blind. Strong hand with excellent pot odds (3.5:1). Against Minni's button raise, this hand has strong equity and plays well postflop. Already have action from Elon in small blind, so a flop is coming. Stack is healthy at 9900. This is a standard profitable call with good playability postflop.

Preflop, K5s was a reasonable call OOP against a minraise from the button with good playability, suited, king blocker, and deep stacks. Flop 6c3cQh misses me completely: king-high with no pair, no flush draw (spades), no straight draw (gutshot unlikely). Board features a queen, two clubs providing flush draw outs, low cards. Acting first OOP with air/no showdown value in a bloated 900 pot against aggressive Minni (BTN wide range possible: Qx, suited aces, broadways, Ax suited clubs) and loose BB Claude. Betting risks folding out better but getting called by worse rarely; bluffs inefficient here as opponents fold too often to polarized SB lead or call draws/value. Best play: check to control pot, induce bluffs/mistakes postflop, realize equity by check-calling if minbet or check-folding aggression. Folding impossible as checked to me.

On the flop with As9h on a board of 6c3cQh, I'm in the big blind with 9700 chips. Both opponents have checked to me, giving me the betting initiative. 

Hand strength analysis: I hold ace-high with a gutshot (any K makes a straight). While not a strong made hand, this has decent equity and showdown value against typical button/small blind calling ranges.

Key factors:
1. Initiative: Both opponents checked, which gives me control
2. Board texture: Dry flop (6c3cQh) doesn't strongly connect to most typical raising ranges
3. Pot position: 900 chips with good stack depth
4. Opponent tendencies: Minni's button raise is typically wide, Elon's cold call suggests a decent hand

Betting strategy: A ~350 chip bet (roughly 40% of pot) is standard here. This:
- Takes down the pot immediately if opponents fold
- Builds a pot when I have reasonable equity
- Maintains good sizing for position and stack management
- Applies controlled aggression without overcommitting

This is a standard continuation-style bet with decent equity and solid risk/reward.

Flop: 6♣ 3♣ Q♥. I hold 6♠4♣ — I have a pair of sixes (a vulnerable second pair) with a weak kicker. Claude leads for 350 into a 1,250 pot (~28% pot). Calling 350 gives me ~21.9% pot odds to continue, which is reasonable vs a wide c-bet range from the big blind. I have position, showdown value, and the bet is small enough that raising would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and risk a re-raise or being crushed by Qx/sets. Fold is too conservative because my pair has value and will often be good at showdown. Plan: call 350 to see the turn, keep worse hands and bluffs in, and re-evaluate on the next card (play cautiously to further aggression; extract value or raise only if I improve significantly).

Preflop call with K5s suited was standard OOP vs BTN minraise for playability and pot control. Flop 6c3cQh completely misses: king-high, no pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), no straight draw. Already checked, now facing BB c-bet of 350 (into ~1000 pot) and BTN flat-call from original raiser Minni, who shows aggression but here elects to call rather than raise. Pot ~1600, calling 350 gives ~4.5:1 odds (~18% equity), but I have negligible equity vs ranges: Claude (BB defender) likely Qx, mid pairs, club flush draws (e.g., AcXc); Minni (BTN raiser caller) strong: Qx, sets, better pairs/draws. King-high rarely good, no showdown value OOP. Raising bluff bad: poor fold equity vs two players (Claude protects, Minni traps value), stacks deep but committed ranges crush me. Calling OOP multiway spews chips with air, induces river trouble. Fold preserves stack, waits for better spots in 150-hand tourney.

Checking on the turn with As9h on 6c 3c Qh 7c. I have ace-high with a gutshot straight draw - too weak to barrel again out of position against Minni who already called my flop raise. Checking allows me to keep the pot manageable, gather information about her hand strength, and potentially improve on the river. Betting again would be a marginal bluff that likely gets called by better hands.

On the turn I have 6♠4♣ on 6♣ 3♣ Q♥ 7♣: currently a single pair (pair of sixes) with a weak kicker, plus a 4‑card club draw. Unique outs to improve on the river: 9 clubs + 3 non‑club fives + 2 remaining sixes = 14 outs ≈ 14/46 ≈ 30.4% to improve to a straight/flush/two‑pair/trips. Claude led the flop then checked the turn — that line often indicates pot control with a medium-strength hand (Qx) or a missed/turn draw rather than a clear monster. Betting now would bloat the pot with a vulnerable second pair and will frequently be called by hands that currently beat me (Qx) or get raised by the nuts. Checking preserves stack, realizes our equity for a reasonable price, and lets us re‑evaluate on the river (bet for value if we improve, fold/call based on villain’s action if we don’t). Therefore I check behind.

River with As9h on 6c3cQh7cJh - ace-high with no made hand. Minni already called my flop bet, indicating some strength. The turn was checked through, suggesting both players missed or are cautious. Betting would be a pure bluff into an opponent who showed interest - not a profitable strategy. Checking allows me to see Minni's action or take a showdown with legitimate ace-high value. This is standard river play out of position with marginal equity.

Final board: 6♣ 3♣ Q♥ 7♣ J♥. I hold 6♠4♣ — only a single pair of sixes with a weak kicker; my turn club draw missed on the river. Claude was the preflop caller and then led the flop (350) — a line consistent with medium-strength Qx, value hands, or some bluffs — and then checked both the turn and the river. Given that line, a lot of his calling/raising range on the flop still beats my pair (top pair Qx, overpairs, some pocket pairs); the hands I would get value from on the river (pure air or very weak single-pair hands) are thin and not likely to call a bet. Bluffing here has limited fold equity against a player who showed aggression on the flop and can easily cost me a sizable portion of my stack for a small chance of success. Pot is 1,600 and my stack is 9,400 — there is no reason to bloat the pot with a marginal holding. Best play: check and take showdown (realize equity) rather than bluff or overbet for thin value.

UTG with pocket 3s (3c3h) at a 4-handed table and deep stacks (~110bb). Small pairs play best when raised (isolate + fold equity) or folded rather than limped multiway. I’ll open to a standard 3x (300) to take the initiative, deny good odds to the blinds, and keep a clear plan to set-mine if called. If I face a large, polarized 3-bet I’ll fold more often; I’ll call smaller 3-bets if implied odds justify it and re-evaluate postflop.

75o is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, straight potential, or suited value. Facing a UTG raise from Minni, who has shown some raising frequency but still represents strength from early position. On the button, even with position, calling 300 into a 450 pot offers poor implied odds and playability post-flop. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots in a tournament setting where survival and stack maximization are key.

7c 6s is a weak offsuit hand with poor equity against Minni's early position raise to 300. While I'm only 250 to call, I'm out of position and likely trailing against a UTG raiser. At this stage of the tournament (hand 12 of 150), with a healthy 9300 stack, it's prudent to fold weak hands and preserve chips rather than chase marginal situations. Elon folding from the button also suggests Minni's raise had strength. Flash in the big blind could also enter the pot, making this a multiway pot which further reduces my equity with 7-6o. Folding is the mathematically sound decision.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Four offsuit. Minni has raised to 300 from UTG in a 4-handed game. T4o is a very weak hand that realizes equity poorly, especially out of position against a range that should be relatively strong coming from UTG. Even though I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call a 450 pot), this hand is a clear fold to avoid being put in difficult situations post-flop with a dominated hand.

T8o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability from UTG in a 4-handed game. Early position requires a strong range to open or limp. Folding preserves stack and avoids difficult postflop situations with dominated hands or draws unlikely to improve well. Opponents have shown some aggression in spots, but no need to speculate with trash preflop. Deep stacks favor tight play early in tournament.

I'm on the button facing a decision with 4c3c after Elon (UTG) folded. While position is excellent and I'm only facing two opponents, 4-3 offsuit is one of the weakest holdings in poker with minimal playability.

Analysis:

- Pot odds: 150 to call 100 (1.5:1), needing 40% equity to break even
- Against two likely ranges, this hand is significantly below breakeven

Throughout this tournament, I've been selective with my plays - folding marginal hands like T5o, 7-2o, and 8-2o in early positions. This same discipline should apply here. Position advantage doesn't override fundamental hand quality when facing two opponents.

The optimal play is to fold and preserve chips for stronger situations where my button position can truly add value.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Ace offsuit (AKo), which is a premier starting hand. Since both UTG (Elon) and the Button (Claude) have folded, the action is on me to either call or raise against the Big Blind (Minni). Raising is the optimal play here to extract value and potentially take down the pot immediately or build it for post-flop play where I have a strong advantage. I will raise to 3.5x the big blind to 350.

Heads-up with deep stacks and I hold A♦4♠ — a playable, high-equity hand and an Ace-blocker. Calling the extra 250 (350 total committed) is the +EV line: pot odds (~26.7%) are favorable versus a wide SB open and calling keeps the pot controlled and allows postflop play. A min-reraise to 600 is too small to fold out much of his range and would put me OOP with a marginal hand, so I’ll call and proceed based on the flop (continue on A/4 or good draw textures, fold to heavy action on dry boards without improvement).

I am the pre-flop aggressor with AKo. The flop is Qc 5h 6h, which is somewhat neutral. I missed the board, but I still have two overcards and the range advantage as the small blind raiser. I will place a continuation bet of 350 (50% of the pot) to exert pressure on the Big Blind. This bet targets hands in her range that missed the flop or are weak draws, while also allowing me to see where I stand if she calls or raises.

Flop: Qc 5h 6h. I hold Ad 4s — Ace-high only, but I have an Ace-blocker and a backdoor wheel (four 3s). Flash’s c‑bet 350 into a 1050 pot makes calling 350 cost me to try to win a ~1750 pot: break‑even equity is ~20%. Versus a wide SB opening/c‑betting range my hand has roughly that equity plus implied odds, and I have position. Raising here is too thin — I don’t have a made hand and won’t fold out many pairs or strong draws, and I don’t want to inflate the pot with marginal equity. I’ll call 350 to keep the pot controlled, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the turn (continue/raise if I pair or hit the 3, fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss).

The turn card is the 5 of spades, which pairs the board. I am currently holding Ace-high (AKo). While I was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, Minni's call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board, likely a Queen, a 6, or a heart draw. The pairing of the 5 doesn't significantly help my hand, and continuing to bet into a range that called the flop might be overplaying my hand. By checking, I can control the size of the pot, see how Minni reacts, and potentially get to a cheap showdown or hit an Ace or King on the river. If she checks behind, I get a free card; if she bets, I can evaluate based on the size.

Flash opened wide from the SB and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn after the 5 paired. That check is more indicative of weakness or pot‑control (Qx, Kx, missed heart draws, weak pairs) than of a value hand like trips. I hold Ad4s: I have almost no showdown value but I do have an Ace‑blocker which reduces some of Flash’s strongest Q/A combinations. The pot is 1,400 — a bet of ~700 (~50% pot) requires ~33% fold frequency to be a +EV bluff, which is easily achievable given his turn check and the board texture. Betting here puts pressure on his checking range, can take the pot immediately, and avoids going to a cheap showdown with only Ace‑high. If he raises I can fold (I have little value); if he calls I can re‑evaluate on the river. I’ll apply pressure with a medium half‑pot bet.

I am currently holding Ace-high (AKo) on a board of Qc-5h-6h-5s. I was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, but Minni called both times, indicating she likely has a piece of the board (a Queen, a six, or a draw). On the turn, the five pairs. I checked to control the pot, and Minni bet 700 into a 1400 pot (offering 3:1 odds). To call, I would need approximately 25% equity. With only two cards to hit (an Ace or a King), I have roughly a 13% chance of improving to top pair on the river, assuming an Ace or King even wins the hand. Given Minni's betting action on a paired board, she likely has at least a Queen or a five. Since I am behind her made hands and do not have the proper odds to chase my outs, I will fold to preserve my stack.

I'm dealt K9o from UTG, which is a weak hand to play from an early position. I am on the button with 6-3 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. While the button is an ideal position for stealing blinds, this specific hand has almost no playability if called. Heads-up preflop in the small blind with Q♦4♠. Against a single big blind I should open a very wide range to steal the blind and play postflop in position. Q4s is playable here and has enough playability to raise rather than limp. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call smaller 3-bets selectively, and C-bet/fold or C-bet/realize depending on flop texture. Raising to 300 maximizes immediate EV while keeping future options flexible.

Facing a standard SB raise to 300 after UTG and Button folds, pot is 400, calling an additional 200 for about 3:1 odds. Ts8c is a speculative offsuit hand with straight potential and decent playability. SB opening range from late position after folds is wide (likely 40-50% of hands), and I have position heads-up postflop. Early in the tournament (hand 14/150), defending the BB wide is standard to realize equity and counter stealing attempts. Folding is too tight; raising lacks value/protection with this hand. Call to see flop.

I opened preflop to 300 and got one caller from the big blind. Flop 8♦-9♠-2♣ is very dry and disconnected. I hold Q♦4♠ — just Q-high with essentially no backdoor flush (only one diamond on board and only one in my hand), and only a few combos in villain’s range have straight draws. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot to maximize fold equity and leave room to fold to a large raise — Q4 has little showdown value if opposed. Bet 200 into the 600 pot; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn, and fold to a sizable raise.

Facing a 200 bet into an 800 pot (pot odds ~4:1) on a dry 8d9s2c flop OOP as BB vs SB. Raising risks bloating pot OOP with marginal strength and vulnerable to 4-bet shoves; better to call, pot control, realize equity on turn/river, and re-evaluate. Minni's small bet sizing suggests wide range, allowing cheap realization of hand.

Pot is 1000 and I hold Q♦4♠ on 8♦-9♠-2♣-5♣. I have very little showdown value, but as the preflop aggressor I have a range advantage and can credibly represent pairs or strong draws. A half-pot second barrel (~500) maximizes fold equity against many of Elon’s flop-calling hands (broadway misses, Kx/Ax, some weaker pairs) while keeping the pot manageable if I’m called. Plan: apply pressure with a 500 bet and fold to a sizable raise; if called I’ll reassess on the river.

On the turn facing a 500 bet into 1500 (~1/3 pot) from SB Minni, who opened wide preflop and c-bet small flop. Board 8d9s2c5c is drawy now with club flush draw and straight possibilities. My Ts8c gives me pair of 8s (second pair) + open-ended straight draw (7 or J for nut straight, 8 outs). Deep stacks (90bb+), position advantage as BB, early tournament favors controlled aggression. Minni's history shows turn aggression (e.g., Hand 13 won with turn bet), but wide SB range vulnerable. Raise to 1500 (pot-relative ~3x her bet), building pot while leaving room for river play. Folding/checking too weak.

Fold. I opened, c-bet a dry 8♦-9♠-2♣ flop and barreled the 5♣ turn with Q♦4♠, but I never connected — I only have Q-high and very little backdoor equity. I am UTG in a 4-handed game. Jack-Five suited (Js 5s) is a weak hand that does not meet the requirements for a standard opening range from this position. Preflop in the small blind with 4d Tc, an offsuit hand with poor connectivity and low playability. Folding 9-4 offsuit from the Big Blind. While I'm getting approximately 3.25:1 odds to call (need to call 200 to win 650), 9-4o is simply too weak of a hand to continue with at this stage. UTG with pocket 7s in a 4-handed game and ~110 BB effective — this is a hand I want to open-raise for value and fold equity rather than limp. I'll raise to 3x the big blind (300). Preflop on the button with 2sJd, an offsuit trash hand with poor playability, no straight or flush potential, and a large gap between cards. Folding 56o (5c 6h) from the small blind in this spot is the clear choice. This is one of the weakest hands in poker - unconnected cards with limited playability. Key factors:

I currently hold the second-nut flush with the King of diamonds on a board showing four diamonds. The only hand that beats me is a hand containing the Ace of diamonds. I hold the second-nut flush with the King of diamonds on a board with four diamonds. The only hand that beats me is the Ace of diamonds.
