A bet of roughly 60% of the pot is standard here.

Heads‑up vs a wide SB open. With 4♦7♥ on K♦5♠6♠ I have an 8‑out straight draw (any 3 or 8) — ~17% to hit on the turn, ~31% by the river. Pot is 950 and it costs 350 to continue; calling makes the pot ~1300 so break‑even equity is ~27% (350/1300). My ~31% by‑river equity + implied odds makes a call +EV. Raising as a semi‑bluff isn’t attractive: villain’s range contains lots of Kx and calling/3‑betting frequency is high, and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with just a draw and ~49 BB effective. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to big turn aggression unless I improve, and extract value if I complete the straight.

The Queen of Spades is a dangerous card as it completes a potential flush and also improves hands like KQ or QJ with a spade. While I still have top pair with my King, the board has become significantly more coordinated and dangerous. Checking is the most prudent play here; it allows me to control the pot size, avoid a check-raise from a made flush, and see how Minni responds. I can evaluate whether to call a bet based on her sizing or potentially see a cheap river if she checks behind with a draw or a weaker pair.

I hold 4♦7♥ on 5♠ K♦ 6♠ Q♠ — I completely missed and only have 8 outs (any 3 or 8), ~17% to hit on the river. Pot is 1,300 and Flash checked the turn. Betting here would bloat the pot out of position with a weak draw and will frequently get called by Kx/Qx or a pair that beats me; I have no reason to build the pot now. Checking preserves my stack, gives me a free river card, and keeps the option to bluff or value-bet if I actually complete the straight. Plan: check the turn; if I hit the river (3/8) bet for value, otherwise fold to significant aggression or consider a river bluff only in the right texture.

On the river, the board pairs the King, giving me Three of a Kind (Kings). The turn check from both players suggests that Minni likely does not have a flush, as she would have been expected to bet for value on a coordinated board after I showed weakness by checking. By betting now, I can extract value from a Queen, a pocket pair like 77-JJ, or potentially a weaker King that she was playing cautiously. A bet of 650, which is half the pot, is large enough to get value but small enough to still look like a possible missed draw or a thin value bet, encouraging her to call with her marginal holdings.

I hold 4♦7♥ on 5♠ K♦ 6♠ Q♠ K♣ and completely missed my straight/flush. Flash (preflop raiser) bets 650 into the 1,300 pot (pot now 1,950). Calling 650 would commit me to win a 2,600 pot (after my call), so I need ~25% equity (650/2600) to break even. I have no pair and no realistic outs — my draw missed — so I have essentially 0% to improve. Flash’s line (open preflop, c-bet flop, check turn, small river bet) is consistent with thin value (Kx/trips, KQ, some Qx) or a probe that will beat my missed holding far more often than not. Per my turn plan (check and fold river if I didn’t improve), and given the unfavorable pot odds and range, the correct play is to fold and preserve stack. Raising as a bluff is too risky out of position with no blockers and would bloat the pot unnecessarily.

I am on the button with 53o, which is a very weak hand. While I have a significant chip lead, Minni and Claude have stacks that are still deep enough to defend their blinds. Given the low strength of my hand, there is no reason to attempt a steal or enter the pot. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should be opening very wide to steal the big blind and take the initiative. J♣6♠ is marginal but perfectly reasonable to raise from the SB in heads-up since I’ll have position post-flop. My stack (≈45.5bb) and Claude’s deep stack mean a standard min-open is appropriate to preserve fold equity without bloating the pot. I’ve already posted 50, so I’ll add 150 more to make a 2bb open (200 total). Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call or continue to the flop if Claude calls, and use position to c-bet selectively or check and control the pot when behind.

Minni has raised to 200 from the small blind, and I'm in the big blind with 7h6d. I need to call just 100 more with excellent pot odds (3:1). While 76o is not a premium hand, the mathematical odds justify a call. Minni has been playing aggressively throughout this session (raising frequently from early positions), and this could easily be a raising steal attempt from the small blind. With such favorable odds and position in the small blind with a weak raise, I should call to see the flop. If the flop doesn't connect favorably, I can fold. My 9750 stack is deep enough to take this flop profitably.

I have top pair (J♣ on board with J♥ in hand) but a very weak 6 kicker and an A on the flop that beats me. As the preflop aggressor in heads-up with position, a continuation bet is standard to get value from worse Jx hands, QJ/KJ-type holdings and to charge backdoor draws. I’ll size to about half the pot (250 into 400) to extract value while keeping the pot manageable if I’m up against an Ace. Plan: if Claude folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn (exercise caution on A/K/club turns and consider pot control or check-calling versus aggression); if he raises big I’ll give heavy weight to Ax or a set and fold to large pressure, calling smaller raises occasionally to realize equity.

The flop has completely missed my hand. I have 76 with no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw on an Js 5c Ac board. Minni's flop raise strongly suggests strength (likely an Ace, Jack, or high pair), and my hand has minimal equity against his range. While the pre-flop call was justified by pot odds, the board texture makes continuing hopeless. Folding preserves my stack for more favorable situations.

I’m on the button with 8♣4♠ — a weak, easily-dominated hand but I have position and decent pot odds (pot 150, cost 100). Both opponents are deep-stacked and Flash has shown frequent aggression/3-bet capacity, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand by raising. Calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me use position to see a flop and either take a free card or exercise fold equity on favorable textures. Plan: call 100, evaluate the flop — c-bet small if it checks through on a favorable board or fold to heavy action with no equity.

With pocket queens in small blind position, I should raise to build the pot and apply pressure preflop. Simply calling 50 more chips would be passive with such a premium hand. A raise to 350 total (300 more on top of the 50 call) represents solid aggression that extracts value from weaker holdings and defines my hand strength. This sizing maintains fold equity while keeping my stack flexible for postflop play.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Four offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni limped from the button and Claude raised to 350 from the Small Blind. Given the raise and my poor hand strength, calling 250 chips out of position is not worth the risk, even with my significant stack lead. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I initially limped intending to see a flop, but Claude’s raise to 350 changes the decision. 8♣4♠ is a very weak, easily-dominated hand with poor equity versus a raiser from the small blind. Calling an extra 250 doesn’t give favorable pot odds and a thin 3‑bet bluff is unnecessary here—I’d rather preserve my ~47bb stack for better spots in position. Fold.

8-5 offsuit is a weak hand that doesn't justify a call even from the button. While button position is strong, the hand quality is below my threshold for profitability. Looking at my recent history, I've been appropriately selective with weak hands. At 50/100 blinds with a 9850 stack, I should preserve chips for stronger opportunities. Minni is short (4600) and Flash is deep (25400), but neither factor changes the fact that 8-5o is a fold. Better to wait for a premium hand to capitalize on my position.

I hold Queen-Jack offsuit in the Small Blind. Since the Button has folded, it's a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. QJo is a strong hand in this spot, and I have a significant chip lead over Minni. I will raise to 300 to apply pressure and take control of the pot pre-flop.

I'm in the big blind with 9h8c facing a 300 open from the small blind. My stack is ~46BB so I'm deep enough to realize equity postflop, and the caller price is attractive: I only need to put in 200 more to call into a 400 pot (roughly 3:1 pot odds). 9-8o is playable against a wide SB opening and can flop well (pairs, straights, backdoor draws). I don't want to 3-bet OOP with this hand — a reraise to 500 is thin and will commit more chips out of position against a large-stacked, frequently-opening opponent. Folding would be too tight given the pot odds. Plan: call to see the flop, continue aggressively if I connect (pair/strong draw), and exercise pot control / fold to heavy action when I miss.

I hold Queen-Jack offsuit on a paired board of Tc-7d-7s. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage on this board. Most of Minni's calling range from the Big Blind consists of hands that missed this flop or are marginal pairs. A continuation bet of 50% pot should be sufficient to fold out most of her air and maintain my initiative in the hand, especially given my significant stack lead.

Flop: Tc 7d 7s. I have 9h8c — no pair but an open-ended straight draw (needs a 6 or J). That’s 8 outs (~31–32% to improve by the river). Flash bets 300 into a 900 pot; calling 300 is cheap: break-even equity is roughly 20% (300 to win a ~1500 final pot), so my draw has positive expected value to call. Flash opened preflop and is c-betting a small sizing, so his range is wide — calling keeps the pot controllable OOP and lets me realize equity with good implied odds if I hit. Raising OOP as a semi-bluff is possible but thin against a deep, aggressive opponent and inflates the pot unnecessarily. Plan: call 300, see the turn; if I complete the straight I’ll extract value, if I miss and face heavy turn pressure I’ll fold to avoid committing too many chips.

The turn has brought a third seven, making the board 10-7-7-7. With three sevens on the board, any player holding the case seven would have quad sevens. If no one has a seven, the hand is determined by the highest cards accompanying the trip sevens. My hand (Q-J) gives me a best five-card hand of 7-7-7-Q-10. This loses to any Ten (full house), any Ace-high, or any King-high. Given that Minni called both pre-flop and on the flop, she could easily have a Ten, an Ace, or a pocket pair. I will check here to control the size of the pot and see how she reacts, as my hand is currently a marginal one on this specific texture.

I missed my straight on the turn (board: Tc 7d 7s 7c). I have no pair and essentially zero showdown value, while Flash checked — which could be weakness or a slow-play/river-trap with a Ten or the lone 7. Betting or raising OOP here is too thin: I’d either be calling a check-raise into a big pot or folding to a river reaction with no equity. I’ll check to control the pot, see the river for free, and only continue if I improve to the straight (then extract value) or face a very small bluff; otherwise I’ll fold to meaningful aggression.

The board has three sevens and a Ten. My best five-card hand is 7-7-7-Q-10. This is a solid hand, but it is vulnerable. I lose to any Ten (full house), the case Seven (quads), any Ace (7-7-7-A-10), and any King (7-7-7-K-10). Given that Minni called pre-flop and on the flop, her range includes many Ace-high and King-high hands, as well as some Tens. After the turn checked through, I have no reason to believe she is particularly weak or strong. Checking the river is the safest play to reach showdown or control the pot size, as betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands and will almost certainly be called by hands that beat me (like Ax or Kx).

I checked the turn and hit my straight on the river (6d → I now have 6-7-8-9-10). Flash checked the river, which can be either a weak showdown hand or a slow-play/trap with a full house/quads (any Ten, any Six, any pocket pair or the lone 7 beat me). Betting small extracts value from worse hands that will call (overcards, missed draws, some single-pair type lines that didn’t improve) while limiting my loss if I get check-raised by a full house. Pot is 1,200 — a modest ~1/3-pot bet keeps pressure on marginal hands and preserves fold equity vs a raise. Plan: bet a small amount for value (and fold to a clear check-raise, since a raise almost always signifies a full house/quads that beats my straight).

The board has tripled the sevens, making the best five-card hand trips with the two highest kickers available. My best hand is 7-7-7-Q-10. This hand beats any pocket pair below Tens (like 8-8 or 9-9, as their best hand would be 7-7-7-10-9 or 7-7-7-10-8), and any hand with a kicker lower than a Queen. I lose to any Ace (7-7-7-A-10), any King (7-7-7-K-10), any Ten (full house T-T-7-7-7), 6-6 (full house 6-6-7-7-7), or the case seven (quads). Given the small bet of 400 into a 1200 pot, I am getting 4:1 odds to call. In a Small Blind vs Big Blind battle, it is plausible Minni is betting a worse hand or a pocket pair I now beat, or simply bluffing. The price is right to call and see her hand.

I am on the button with Ace-King suited, a premium hand. In a 3-handed game, this hand is extremely strong. I am the chip leader by a significant margin. I will make a standard raise to 300 chips to build the pot and exert pressure on the blinds.

I’m in the small blind with 6♦5♣ facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to call (min-raise to 500). 6-5 offsuit is a very marginal hand — low, unsuited, and frequently dominated by the button’s raising range. Calling 250 to play out of position against both the opener and the big blind forces me into difficult multiway or OOP heads-up spots where my equity is poor. Pot odds (~1.8:1, ~36% equity needed) don’t justify calling here against a standard button range, and a 3-bet with this holding would be overly aggressive and spew chips unnecessarily. With ~56bb effective and plenty of hands left in the tournament, preserving my stack and waiting for a better spot is the right play. I will fold.

Folding A2o (Ace-Deuce offsuit) from the big blind. While pot odds are decent at 2.25:1, the hand is weak with poor kicker strength. More critically, I'll be out of position post-flop against Flash who has position and a large chip lead. Early in tournament (hand 27/150), preserving chips for better spots is standard strategy. A2o out of position is a marginal situation with negative expected value post-flop playability.

Button with Qc3s — weak but playable because I have position and the pot is small (150). Standard tactic here is to open-raise to take the initiative and fold out the blinds often. I’ll raise to 300 (a typical 3x open) to apply pressure; if I face a sizable 3‑bet I’ll fold this hand (dominated and poor kicker), but if I get called I’ll play post‑flop in position, c-betting favorable textures and controlling pot size on overcards/dangerous boards. Calling 100 gives the big blind excellent odds and misses the chance to steal, so raise is higher EV.

Calling 250 more with TsJc from the small blind against a short-stacked button raiser. Getting 2:1 pot odds into a likely wide range from Minni, with strong post-flop position. TJ plays well in position against a probable wide button-raising range. Folding loses the blind, raising is unnecessary with this equity. Call is optimal.

I am in the Big Blind with 92o. Minni raised to 300 from the Button and Claude called from the Small Blind. Although I have good pot odds to call (200 to win 700), 92o is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Playing this hand out of position against two opponents is likely to lead to losing more chips post-flop. I will prioritize protecting my large chip lead and fold.

Betting 400 with TsJc on QsAs3d. I'm acting first post-flop with position in a heads-up situation. I have strong equity with K-high broadway draw (4 outs), 9-high straight draw (4 outs), and flush draw outs (~8 additional spades). That's approximately 15-16 total outs giving me ~33-35% equity. Betting here represents strength, allows me to take down the pot, and controls action against a short-stacked opponent. A 400 c-bet is standard sizing at 57% of the 700 pot.

Flop: Qs As 3d. My hand Qc3s is two pair (Queens and Treys) — a very strong holding on this texture. Claude’s 400 into a 1100 pot is a small bet that will be called by lots of worse hands (single Ace, Qx, medium pairs, draws), while only a few combos beat me (AA/QQ/33 and the rare A3/AQ two‑pair combos). I want to extract value and deny free cards, and keep the initiative in position. I make a value raise to the minimum allowed (to 800 total) to charge worse pairs and Qx, protect against backdoor draws, and keep the pot manageable if Claude shows heavy resistance; I’ll re-evaluate on the turn if he applies big 3‑bet pressure.

Calling 400 with Ts Jc on Qs As 3d facing Minni's check-raise. I have approximately 15-16 outs (8 straight outs + 7-8 flush outs) for ~33-35% equity. Pot odds of 5.75:1 are very favorable, requiring only ~15% equity to break even. With two cards remaining, drawing equity is maximized. Minni's check-raise likely indicates AA, QQ, or AK-type holdings where I'm either in a race or have significant equity to improve. Calling is +EV and allows me to realize my draw equity.

Betting 850 on the turn with TsJc after Minni checks. I have a premium draw with ~15-16 outs (straight and flush possibilities) providing 35-40% equity. This bet size (37% of pot) maintains aggressive pressure, extracts value from her likely weaker holdings, and forces her short-stacked opponent into a difficult decision with one card remaining. Position and initiative are strong here after she checked, so taking control is +EV.

I have Qc3s — top two pair (Q+3). Claude's 850 into a 3150 pot is a very small turn bet. Many hands that beat me (AQ, A3, AA, QQ, 33) exist but are relatively few; a lot of his continuing range (single A, worse Qx, medium pairs, draws) are behind. Calling 850 gives ~21% break‑even equity, keeps worse hands and draws in, and controls pot size. Raising to 1700 would bloat the pot and can get me check‑raised or committed by the few stronger combos, so I’ll call and re‑evaluate the river (extract value if it’s safe, fold to polarized river overbets).

Checking the river with Ts Jc on a final board of Qs As 3d 5h 3h. My hand is just ace-high (A-Q-J-T-5), completely missed all draws. Minni's flop check-raise and turn call indicate a made hand, likely a pair that beats my ace-high. Betting is valueless here—I have no realistic win equity. Checking allows me to reach showdown without inflating the pot and losing additional chips on a losing hand. This conserves chips for stronger situations ahead.

I hold Qc3s and the river 3h gives me a full house (three 3s plus two Queens). This is a very strong hand but not the absolute nuts — AA, QQ, 33 or A3 can beat me. Claude’s line (bet on flop, called my raise, a small bet on turn, then checking the river) more often maps to Ax, Qx or medium hands checking to control the pot; a check on the river is more likely caution than a guaranteed trap. With the pot at 4,000 and my stack 3,700 I want to extract thin value from worse hands (single A, single Q, some second-best two-pair combos) while avoiding bloating the pot and giving the rare stronger full-house/quads owners a chance to check-raise me off my hand. A small-to-medium value bet (~1.2k ≈ 30% pot) is the best compromise: it prices in worse hands to call, keeps the pot manageable if Claude shows strength, and avoids an overcommittal shove. I will bet 1,200 to get value from worse hands and fold to a large, polarized check-raise if it shows up.

I hold Ts Jc on a final board of Qs As 3d 5h 3h, making an ace-high hand (A-Q-J-T-5). The river 3h completes the board to have a pair of threes, which is unlikely to have improved my hand while very likely to have strengthened Minni's range. Pot odds are 6400:1200 (5.33:1), requiring approximately 15.8% equity to break even. Against Minni's line of pre-flop raise → flop aggression → turn call → river bet, she almost certainly holds a made hand (pair of aces, queens, threes, or possibly a small pocket pair turned two pair). My ace-high has minimal equity in this spot—likely 5-10% at best. Even with the assumption of occasional river bluffs, her consistently strong action suggests genuine strength, not a bluff. Calling 1200 chips would be -EV. Folding preserves chips for stronger opportunities ahead. Clear fold.

Queen-Deuce offsuit is a weak unpaired hand with poor connectivity and limited postflop potential. While I'm in position on the button with favorable immediate pot odds (calling 100 to win 150), this hand doesn't warrant a call even with positional advantage. The risk of facing additional aggression from Flash or Minni, both of whom have shown active tendencies in recent hands, outweighs the benefit of position with such a weak holding. I need to remain selective and preserve my 7,800-chip stack for stronger opportunities where I have better equity. Folding weak hands from the button is standard fundamentally sound poker.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Eight offsuit. Claude has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Minni). In a three-handed game, K8o is a strong hand heads-up against the Big Blind's range. I have a significant chip lead and want to put pressure on Minni, who has a medium stack. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to either win the pot immediately or take control of the hand going into the flop.

I’m in the big blind with 9♦4♣ — a very weak, offsuit hand with poor playability. Flash opened to 300 from the small blind/button and will have positional advantage postflop, and he’s shown frequent preflop aggression and large stacks to pressure with. Although calling 200 is a relatively small price, playing this hand out of position will put me in many marginal spots where I’m likely dominated or forced to fold later; a raise would be a thin bluff that can easily be 3-bet. I’ll conserve chips and fold to wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with Ace-Eight offsuit in a 3-handed game. This is a strong opening hand. With my significant chip lead over Minni and Claude, I should apply pressure. I will raise to 300, which is my standard opening size, to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop.

I'm in the small blind with Qd3d facing a Button open to 300. Pot is 450 and I need to put in 250 more (300 total). Q3s is a marginal, often-dominated hand out of position, but the call is cheap relative to my 7,550 stack and helps defend against frequent BTN steals. 3-betting with this hand isn't attractive — a min 3-bet to 500 won't fold many BTN hands and a larger 3-bet overcommits with a weak holding. Plan: call to see a flop and proceed cautiously OOP — fold to heavy action unless I pair the queen, make two pair/set, or get strong draws; if I improve I can start applying pressure. If BB 3-bets big, I will lean toward folding.

I'm in the big blind with 9♥T♥, a speculative but playable suited connector. Flash raised to 300 from the button, and Minni called from the small blind. The pot is already 700, and I need to call only 200 more, giving me approximately 3.5:1 odds. This is favorable for a hand that can flop well in multiway pots. Raising here would be aggressive with a speculative hand and out-of-position play. My 9-10 of hearts has solid potential for straights and flushes, and with two opponents already invested, a call is the correct play to see the flop. Stack sizes are relatively balanced, so I'm not in desperate need to push or fold marginal holdings.

Check. I have Qd3d — no pair on a 6c-6h-2h board and I’m out of position to two opponents. This flop has very little equity for Q3 (only backdoor diamond possibilities) and is easily dominated by any hand that continues (6x, pocket pairs, any 6, A6, hands with overcards that will c-bet). Best line is pot control and information: check to see what opponents do. I will fold to any meaningful bet (I have no reason to invest OOP with just Q-high), and only consider continuing to a very tiny probe bet (or call a microscopic bet) — otherwise check again and re-evaluate if I pick up a Queen/3 or a favorable turn for a bluff. Preserving stack and avoiding marginal calls OOP is the priority here.

Checking with a strong 15-out draw (flush and straight draws) on a paired flop. Flash will almost certainly continuation bet, allowing me to call with excellent implied odds. This approach maintains pot control while keeping maximum flexibility for turn decisions. With 54% equity and three players, seeing additional cards profitably is the optimal strategy rather than risking stack with an aggressive line.

I have Ace-high on a paired board (6-6-2) in a three-way pot. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage, but checking back here is a prudent play. It allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and avoid a check-raise from a 6 or a small pocket pair. My Ace-high still has significant showdown value in a three-handed game.

Check. I have Q♦3♦ on 6♣-6♥-2♥-K♣ — no pair, no relevant draws, and the K is an overcard that hits a lot of opponents’ continuing ranges. With two players left and out of position, my equity is negligible and a lead-bet/bluff from me is risky and likely to be called by worse or raised by better. Pot control and information are the priorities: check to see if someone barrels (I’ll fold to any meaningful bet and only consider calling a very tiny probe). Preserve stack and look for a cleaner spot on the river.

I'm holding 9♥T♥ on a 6♣ 6♥ 2♥ K♣ turn with a pair of 6s and a strong composite draw:
- Flush draw: 9 hearts remaining
- Straight draw: 8s, Js, Qs provide multiple equity paths (approximately 8 straight outs with some overlap)
- Total outs: ~15-17 accounting for overlaps

Current pot is 900 with Minni having checked, indicating weakness. Flash still has action after me. My stack (7500) is deep enough to take a free river card.

The optimal play here is to CHECK. This line:
1. Keeps both opponents in the pot, maximizing value if I hit
2. Allows me to see the river card cheaply with my strong draw
3. Avoids putting myself in a difficult spot where Flash might reraise over a bet, forcing me to fold my draw
4. Maintains flexibility - if Flash bets, I can call with good odds given the pot size and my outs
5. Preserves stack control given Minni's short-stackedness (7300)

This is a classic "check to call" spot with a strong draw. My ~30-35% equity is best served by seeing one more card at minimal cost, especially in a three-way pot where a check might induce Flash to bet and build the pot for when I hit.

I have Ace-high with an eight kicker on a board of 6-6-2-K. I was the pre-flop aggressor and the flop checked through. On the turn, a King appeared, which is a card that generally favors my range. Both opponents have checked to me again. While I could bet to represent the King, Ace-high still maintains decent showdown value in a three-handed game. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and see the river for free, avoiding a potential check-raise from a player slow-playing a six or holding a small pocket pair.

Three-way checked pot to the river on 6c-6h-2h-Kc-9c. I hold Qd3d with no pair, no club, and essentially zero showdown value against the likely calling ranges (Kx, 9x, 6x, pocket pairs, occasional ace-high). Bluffing into two players on the river has very poor fold equity — I would need both opponents to release hands, which is unlikely given passive checking lines earlier. Pot control and stack preservation dominate here: betting risks chips for a very thin chance of success. I check to reach showdown; if either opponent had bet into me I would fold to any reasonable sizing (no bluff-catch or thin call with Q3).

I'm on the river with 9♥ T♥ and the board has completed as 6♣ 6♥ 2♥ K♣ 9♣. I've made a pair of 9s with a K-T kicker, which is a solid made hand given the action throughout this hand. All three players checked down from flop through turn, and Minni just checked to me on the river, suggesting weakness from both opponents. Flash raised pre-flop but then checked all the way through, indicating either air, a weak pair, or they gave up on their hand. Minni's passive checking also suggests a marginal holding. With a pair of 9s being likely the best hand in this multiway pot where neither opponent has shown strength, a value bet of 500 is appropriate. This sizing (roughly 55% of pot) is standard for extracting value from weaker pairs or drawing hands that bricked, while not overcommitting. This bet should win the pot outright if they fold, or extract value if they call with worse hands like small pairs or ace-high.

I have Ace-high with an eight kicker on a board of 6-6-2-K-9. While Ace-high has some showdown value when the action checks around, Claude has now bet 500 into a 900 pot after Minni checked. In a three-way pot, a bet into two opponents often signifies at least a pair. Since any small pair (like 2s, 3s, 4s, 5s, 7s, or 9s) or any King beats me, calling with Ace-high is too risky. I checked the flop and turn, which might have induced a bluff, but the presence of another player (Minni) makes a bluff less likely for Claude. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Fold. I hold Q♦3♦ with no pair, no club and essentially zero showdown value on 6♣-6♥-2♥-K♣-9♣. Claude checked through earlier streets and now fires a small 500 into a 1,400 pot — a sizing consistent with a thin value bet (Kx, 9x, small pair) or a blocking/steal attempt, but given his passive line the frequency of bluffs is low. Calling 500 to win 1,900 requires ~26% equity; Q3 has far less against his sensible river betting range and I’m out of position. Raising as a bluff is too risky with almost no fold equity and poor equity if called. Per my pre-stated plan to fold to any reasonable bet with this holding, I fold and preserve my 7,300 stack for better spots.

On the button with T♦3♦ and deep stacks (≈73bb) I want to open a wide stealing range. T3s has decent postflop playability (backdoor flush/straight potential) and gains a lot from position. Raising extracts fold equity from the blinds and lets me play the pot in position if called. Folding A6o from the small blind. While I'm getting 1.8:1 odds, the hand is marginal and I'm out of position. Calling 350 on the flop with K9o against Minni's raise. I have top pair (T) with an 8-kicker on 5c-6c-Tc-Js. Calling 675 on the turn with K9o. Receiving 3:1 pot odds against a hand that has 35-40% equity and multiple draw outs (overcards to King, two 9s, straight cards). I have top pair (T8) on 5c‑6c‑Tc‑Js‑Qs but a very vulnerable kicker. Call 550. I am on the button with Ten-Six offsuit. This is a weak starting hand. While I have position, the hand has poor equity and doesn't flop well. Calling 100 more to reach 200 total in the big blind with Ac7s. Facing favorable 3:1 odds and defending my big blind against Minni's aggressive raising pattern. The ace provides decent showdown value, and I can play the flop in position. Total chips committed: 200 (my 100 BB + 100 more to call).

Analyzing the current situation:

- If Minni checks the river, I can check down for showdown with minimal damage
- Betting here would require I push a weak hand into a potentially strong range without clear information

This is a prudent line that preserves chips while keeping options open based on river action.

Betting here would be burning chips on a bluff without sufficient equity or fold equity. Given that I'm already short-stacked in heads-up, I need to preserve chips for spots where I have real value. Checking allows me to see the river card for free—there's a small chance I spike a queen or Flash checks again, avoiding unnecessary chip loss. This is a sound chip preservation play when holding a completely hopeless hand.

The board is Kc 4d 4c 3c 3d, which means there is a double pair (Fours and Threes) with a King kicker on the table. Since neither of my hole cards (7c 6s) is higher than the King or improves the board (to a full house or three of a kind), my best five-card hand is the board itself: K-4-4-3-3. Unless Claude has a King, a Four, or a Three, we will split the pot. Given the checking on all previous streets, it is highly likely we are both playing the board. Betting would not achieve anything as he is unlikely to fold a hand that splits, and I risk being raised if he was slow-playing a monster. Checking is the most sensible action.

With Q7o on a final board of 3c-Kc-4d-3d-4c, I have board value (4-4-3-3-K) but no actual hand strength. Flash has checked through all four streets, indicating weakness or a hand checking down for showdown. With my significantly short stack (10,125 chips vs Flash's 29,675), I should preserve chips rather than bluff-bet on the river into an opponent showing consistent weakness. Checking allows the hand to go to showdown naturally—Flash likely checks back with whatever hand he has, and we resolve the 200-chip pot. This is sound short-stack strategy: avoid unnecessary chip burn on marginal bluffs, and let showdown value play out when opponent is clearly weak. My position on the button doesn't give me fold equity here that would justify a value-thin bluff.

I am on the button with KTo (King-Ten of Clubs), a very strong hand for heads-up play. Claude is the big blind and currently has about 100 big blinds, while I have nearly 300. I want to build the pot and exert pressure. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate to take control of the hand pre-flop.

I'm holding 7d-2d, which is the worst starting hand in poker. I'm in the Big Blind out of position against Flash who has a significant stack advantage (29,475 vs 10,125) and raised from the button. While Flash has been aggressive throughout the tournament, 7-2 is essentially a bluff hand that cannot win at showdown against the vast majority of opponent holdings. Folding from the BB with this hand against an aggressive opponent is clearly the correct play. There's no scenario where calling or raising with the hammer makes sense here.

Heads-up play against Flash with a significant chip deficit (10,075 vs 29,775). I'm on the button/small blind with 9s 6h. Although this is a marginal hand, I'm getting excellent 3:1 odds to call just 50 more chips. In heads-up poker, button position should play a very wide range pre-flop, and with good pot odds and position for the rest of the hand, calling is the standard play. This allows me to see a flop with positional advantage where I can make better decisions based on Flash's action. Folding would unnecessarily concede equity and chips when I'm already short-stacked.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Claude has limped from the Small Blind. While an Ace is often the best hand here, Ace-Deuce doesn't play particularly well against a raising range if I am re-raised, and it's a solid hand to see a free flop with in a heads-up situation. I will check and see the flop.

I have Ace-high on a paired board (9-4-9). Since Claude limped pre-flop and the pot is small, there's no reason to bloat it now. Ace-high has decent showdown value in a limped pot, so I will check to see the turn and observe Claude's action.

Trip nines is a very strong hand on this flop. Flash's check indicates likely weakness. Given my short-stack situation, I need to extract value from this premium hand by betting to build the pot. A 200 chip bet (matching the current pot) is appropriately aggressive while leaving room to respond to Flash's action, and gives me a chance to win immediately or build chips when ahead.

I have Ace-high on a very dry paired board (9-4-9). Claude limped pre-flop and is now betting full pot (200 into 200). This bet size is often used in limped pots to try and end the hand immediately. My Ace-high still has significant showdown value against his range of random overcards or air. While I don't have a piece of the board, folding here to a single bet would be too exploitable. I will call to see the turn and evaluate further, as I may still have the best hand or can improve to top pair.

The King of Clubs on the turn is an overcard that potentially changes the board texture. After calling a pot-sized bet on the flop with Ace-high, I will check to see how Claude reacts to this card. This allows me to control the size of the pot and gather more information about whether he was bluffing the flop or if he has improved his hand.

With trip nines on a 9c 4s 9h Kc board, I have an extremely strong hand that dominates Flash's likely range. Flash's check on the turn indicates weakness - he likely doesn't have a strong made hand. The King likely didn't complete any strong holdings in his range. As a short-stacked player (9,825 chips), I need to maximize value from premium hands. A 500 chip bet builds the pot strategically while allowing Flash opportunities to call with weaker hands like Kx, pocket pairs, or missed draws. This sizing is approximately 5/6 of the current pot and represents standard value betting in this spot. I still have 9,325 chips remaining to navigate the river effectively.

On the turn, the King of Clubs is an overcard that likely connects with part of Claude's limping range (Kx hands). Claude followed up his pot-sized flop bet with a near pot-sized bet (500 into 600) on the turn. My Ace-high has very little equity against a range that includes Kings, nines, or even a four. While he could be double-barreling with air, the price to continue is high, and I have no draw to improve to. Given my significant chip lead, there is no reason to risk chips on a marginal bluff-catch when the board texture has shifted in his favor. I will fold.

I am on the button with Ten-Nine suited, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. With a significant chip lead over Claude, I want to maintain aggression and build the pot. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to exert pressure and take control of the hand.

Q7o is a weak holding from the big blind, even in heads-up play. While pot odds (calling 200 to win 400) might seem tempting, the hand quality is poor and I'll be significantly out of position post-flop. Flash has been aggressive throughout and likely has a wide raising range from the button, but Q7o has limited equity and playability. With a reasonably healthy stack (10,325 chips with 81 hands remaining), I should fold weak hands like this and wait for better opportunities to contest pots. The risk/reward isn't favorable for defending this particular hand.

In heads-up play, position and ace-high hands are valuable. I'm on the button with Ad3d against Flash's big blind. With my chip disadvantage (10,275 vs 29,575), I need to play aggressively to stay competitive. An ace is a strong hand heads-up, and raising from the button with positional advantage is standard. A 2.5x raise to 250 total is appropriate here—strong enough to take control while gathering information about Flash's hand, and not overcommitting given my stack size. This raise maintains aggression while keeping options open postflop where I'll have positional advantage.

I have been dealt pocket Tens, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Claude has raised from the Button (Small Blind) to 250. Since I am in the Big Blind and will be out of position for the rest of the hand, I want to 3-bet for value and to take control of the pot. A standard 3-bet size here is roughly 3x to 4x the opening raise. I will raise to 800 to put pressure on Claude's stack and capitalize on my strong equity.

Flash has 3-bet my button raise to 800, and I'm facing 550 more to call with Ad3d. In heads-up play, Ad3d is a reasonable hand that warrants defending given the pot odds. I have 1,050 chips in the pot and need to call 550 from a stack of 10,075. 

Equity analysis: Ad3d has approximately 35-40% equity against Flash's typical 3-bet range in this heads-up scenario. I'm getting pot odds of roughly 1.9:1, which means I need ~33% equity to break even - my hand meets this threshold with room to spare.

Stack considerations: I'm significantly short-stacked (10,075 vs 28,875), which means I need to accumulate chips strategically rather than fold marginal hands. Calling allows me to see a flop in position with positional advantage for the remainder of the hand.

Ad3d also has favorable characteristics: ace-high is strong heads-up, and I have wheel draw potential (A-2-3-4-5 combos). This gives me multiple ways to improve while maintaining the initiative post-flop from the button.

Folding would be too passive given my chip stack and the hand strength. Raising would be overly aggressive and unnecessary with 550 to call.

I have flopped a set of Tens (middle set), which is a powerhouse hand. The board contains an Ace and a heart draw, which fits well into Claude's 3-bet calling range (Aces like AQ, AJ, or suited connectors). I want to build the pot immediately while my hand is very likely the best. I will lead out with a bet of about 37.5% of the pot (600) to keep his drawing hands and single-pair Aces in the pot, while setting up for a potential shove on later streets given the remaining stack-to-pot ratio.

I'm holding Ad3d on a Th Ah 4s flop after calling Flash's 3-bet preflop. I have top pair (Ace-high) with a 3 kicker. While this is a weak kicker, top pair is a substantial hand in heads-up play. Flash's flop bet of 600 likely represents either a strong made hand (overpair, better ace, or set) or a draw with equity. Analyzing my situation: (1) Pot odds: I'm getting 3.67:1 to call 600 into a 2200 pot—I only need ~21% equity to break even. (2) Hand equity: Against Flash's likely 3-bet range, I estimate my Ad3d has approximately 35-45% equity depending on whether Flash holds better aces, overpairs, or draws. (3) Stack considerations: At 9525 chips, I'm significantly short-stacked, so I need to accumulate chips while maintaining edge. With top pair and favorable equity against his range, folding would be overly passive. Raising all-in with A-3 is unnecessarily aggressive and could commit my entire stack to a marginal situation. Calling allows me to see the turn with positional advantage remaining, while preserving the option to fold if scary cards appear. This approach balances risk management with tournament chip accumulation.

I have a set of Tens (Ts, Td, Th) on a board of Th-Ah-4s-2h. While the Deuce of hearts completes a potential flush, my hand remains extremely strong. I lead the flop for 600, and Claude called. I will continue with a value bet on the turn to charge any single-heart draws or top-pair Ace hands that Claude might be holding. A bet of 1200 (roughly 43% of the pot) is large enough to build the pot for a potential river shove while still being a price that Claude might call with an Ace or a high heart draw. If Claude raises, I have the equity to call and try to fill up to a full house on the river.

Calling Flash's 1200 turn raise with Ad3d. I have top pair with a four-flush draw, providing approximately 25-30% equity and multiple paths to improve on the river (9 diamonds for a flush, 3 threes for trips). Getting 3.33:1 on my chips with break-even equity at ~23%. Given my critical short stack (7.4 big blinds after calling), I cannot afford to fold marginal hands with good equity potential. Top pair plus a solid draw is worth playing heads-up, and if I hit the flush, I'll likely win a significant pot. Folding frequently at this stack depth is a losing strategy in heads-up play.

I have a set of Tens on a board of Th-Ah-4s-2h-Jd. My hand is very strong, but the presence of three hearts on the board is a concern as it completes a potential flush. Claude has called my 3-bet pre-flop and my bets on the flop and turn, which indicates he likely has a strong hand like an Ace (AK, AQ, AJ) or was drawing to the flush. The Jack on the river is mostly a brick, although it does improve AJ to two pair. I will lead with a value bet of 2,600 (50% of the pot). This amount is sized to get called by any Ace in Claude's range while minimizing losses if he has indeed completed a flush. If he raises, I will have to evaluate based on the size, but given the pot odds and the strength of a set, I am unlikely to fold.

Calling Flash's river bet of 2600 with pair of Aces and weak 3-kicker. At 4.1:1 pot odds, I need ~20% equity to break-even. Against Flash's typical heads-up aggressive range, I estimate 25-35% equity with my top pair, easily exceeding the threshold. Heads-up play features frequent bluffs, and Flash's consistent aggression throughout could well represent a bluff. My stack (7,725) remains healthy enough to continue after calling. The pot odds and equity math strongly support this call in a heads-up context.

I hold Jack-Nine suited on the button. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. With Claude having a stack of approximately 50 big blinds, I want to apply pressure and build a pot where I have both the initiative and positional advantage. I will raise to 300, which is my standard opening size.

Raising with premium pair QQ in heads-up against Flash's button raise. With a short stack (5,025), I need to leverage strong hands for chip accumulation. QQ is strong against Flash's likely wide raising range. A 700 total raise applies meaningful pressure while still preserving some flexibility in case Flash comes back over the top. This builds the pot with a hand that's statistically favored in most matchups and demonstrates strength in a heads-up context where aggressive play is essential.

I have Jc9c on the button (heads-up) and I opened for 300. Claude has 3-bet to 700. Claude's stack is very short at 4425 (about 44 BBs), and the pot is already 1000. J9 suited is a very strong hand in heads-up play and plays well post-flop, especially in position. Calling the extra 400 is profitable given the pot odds (400 to win 1000) and the playability of my hand. I will call to see a flop in position.

I'm in the big blind with pocket queens (QQ), and I flopped a set of queens on the Th Qh Js flop - an extremely strong hand. The board is relatively dry with no major connected draws. I'm short-stacked with 4,425 chips remaining after the 1,400 pot, while Flash has 34,175. In a heads-up scenario with a premium made hand and limited chips, I should commit my stack to maximize value. I have a clear hand advantage, and shoving all-in accomplishes multiple goals: (1) it ensures I get full value with my set, (2) it reduces variance in a short-stack situation, and (3) it applies maximum pressure to Flash, who will face a difficult decision with most of his range. Flash's aggressive pre-flop line could include many hands that miss this board entirely or have limited equity against my set.

On a board of Th-Qh-Js, I have middle pair with an open-ended straight draw. However, Claude's 3-bet pre-flop and subsequent overbet shove (4,425 into a pot of 1,400) indicates extreme strength. Claude's range likely includes pocket pairs like AA, KK, QQ, JJ, and TT, or strong broadway hands like AK, AQ, or AJ. Against most of these hands, I am a significant underdog. I need roughly 43% equity to call, but my middle pair is likely beaten, and even hitting an 8 for a straight could lose to a King-high straight if Claude holds AK. Given the stack depths and the size of the bet relative to the pot, the most disciplined play is to fold and maintain my large stack advantage.

With Jack-Queen offsuit on the button against a short-handed opponent, I have a strong hand that plays well in position. My short stack (57 BB) requires proactive play. A standard raise to 300 total chips (250 additional) accomplishes multiple goals: (1) builds the pot with a strong hand, (2) takes control of the hand, (3) may win it preflop against a weaker holding, (4) positions me well for postflop play if called. This is an aggressive but sound play given my stack situation and hand strength in heads-up.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit (As Jh) in the Big Blind. My opponent, Claude, is on the Button and has a relatively short stack of about 55 Big Blinds (5525 chips). AJo is a premier hand in heads-up play and is well ahead of a standard button opening range. I will 3-bet to 900 to take control of the hand, define Claude's range, and put pressure on their stack. If Claude shoves, I will have a tough decision but will likely be priced in to call given my stack advantage and the strength of my hand.

QJ offsuit is a solid hand heads-up against Flash's 3-bet range. With a stack of 5525 and needing to call 600 to compete for a 1200 pot, I'm getting favorable 3:1 pot odds. While my stack is relatively short at ~55 big blinds, calling here allows me to play postflop in position where I can leverage superior hand reading and positioning skills. If I shove, I'm only getting called by better hands (pairs, AK, AQ). By calling, I keep the decision-tree open and can outplay Flash on the flop. QJ has good equity against most of Flash's potential 3-bet holdings and can improve substantially. The position advantage (acting last postflop) is valuable with this hand strength.

On this K-high flop, I retain the range advantage as the 3-bettor, as my range includes strong hands like AK, KQ, and pocket Aces. Although I missed the board with Ace-Jack, a continuation bet is standard here to apply pressure on Claude's range, which likely consists of medium pocket pairs, suited connectors, or weaker Kings. A bet of 600 (one-third of the pot) is efficient; it allows me to fold out hands that have completely missed while keeping the pot manageable given Claude's relatively short stack and potential for aggressive counter-play as seen in previous hands.

On the flop 8h-Kd-5h with QJ, I'm evaluating pot odds and hand equity. I'm getting 4:1 odds (2400 pot, 600 to call). My hand has solid equity: a gutshot straight draw (8 outs to the 10), two overcards (Q and J), and reasonable implied odds given my position postflop. With approximately 32% equity (8-10 outs on two cards), I'm getting favorable odds at 4:1 (needing ~20% equity). While my stack is limited at 4925 chips, calling here allows me to leverage my positional advantage - I act last on the turn and river, which is valuable for hand reading and controlling the betting. If I fold, I abandon the hand and the pot. Flash's 25% pot-sized bet suggests reasonable hand strength but not necessarily a monster, and my position allows me to outplay on future streets. This call is mathematically sound and strategically appropriate given the stack dynamics and position.

The turn pairs the King (Kh), which is a pivotal card. Having 3-bet pre-flop and continuation-bet the flop, I have strongly represented a range that includes Kings (AK, KQ). However, I currently only hold Ace-high. With Claude's remaining stack being quite shallow (4325 chips into a 3000 pot), any further betting essentially commits us both. Checking is the most strategic move here; it allows me to see how Claude reacts to the board pairing. If Claude checks back, I maintain showdown value or can potentially bluff the river if the flush draw misses. If Claude bets, I can evaluate based on sizing, but checking prevents me from over-committing with a hand that is currently just a marginal high-card holding.

Checking is the optimal play here. Flash's flop bet followed by a turn check suggests Flash likely doesn't have a strong King or overpair—if Flash held top pair or better, a bet on the King pairing is very likely. My JdQs has equity with a gutshot to the straight (T), but no made hand. With a short stack (4,325 chips), I should avoid putting chips at risk with a marginal hand. Checking preserves my stack and lets me see the river for free. If the river brings a T for my straight, I can then evaluate whether to bet or check depending on the action. If it's a blank, I'll be in a much better position to fold to a river bet than to commit chips now. This line also keeps my opponent guessing about my hand strength and maintains balance in my play against someone with a 7.5x chip advantage.

The river brings a fourth heart to the board (8h, 5h, Kh, 2h). Holding the Jack of hearts, I have a King-high flush with the Jack as my hole card, making it the third-highest possible flush (trailing only the Ace and Queen of hearts). Given that I was the pre-flop aggressor and the board has paired Kings, my range is perceived as very strong. After checking the turn to control the pot, leading the river for value is the best play. Claude likely has a King or a lower heart that will call a medium-sized bet. I will bet 1,500, which is half the pot, to extract value from these hands. If Claude shoves, I will likely call given the strength of my flush and the pot odds.

River fold with queen-high against Flash's river bet. With JdQs on a board of 8h-Kd-5h-Kh-2h, I have no made hand (just queen-high) on a paired board with three hearts. My hand only beats complete air. Against Flash's 3-bet preflop range that showed flop aggression, I estimate approximately 15-20% equity—below the 25% threshold needed for 3:1 pot odds. The EV of calling is negative (~-865 chips). While my stack is short at 4325 chips, calling a mathematically losing hand accelerates my exit. Better to fold and wait for higher-equity situations with my remaining stack.

I hold J6o on the button (small blind) with a significant chip lead over Claude, who has about 42 big blinds left. While J6o is a marginal hand, in heads-up play, it's worth applying pressure to the shorter stack to pick up the blinds or take control of the pot early. I will put in a standard raise to 300, which has been my consistent sizing, keeping the pressure on Claude while allowing me to fold easily if he decides to shove.

Calling with K4o in the big blind against Flash's button raise. Receiving 3:1 pot odds with a short stack (42 big blinds), which justifies the call despite weak hand strength. Flash has been aggressive throughout the tournament, likely raising a wide range from the button. My King-high has reasonable equity (~32-35%) against his range, exceeding the break-even threshold of 25%. With only ~42 big blinds, I need to be less selective and take equity-positive spots.

Checking with K4o on 7d 6s Qs. This is a weak king-high with no draws against Flash's likely strong range from a button raise. With ~40 big blinds remaining, I'm not yet in push-fold desperation, so bluffing with this hand is not optimal. Checking allows me to see Flash's action and fold if he bets strongly, preserving chips for better spots. If Flash checks back, I gain a free card to improve to two pair or a king-high hand that might win.

I am heads-up against Claude who has a relatively short stack (4025 chips). I hold Jack of Spades and Six of Hearts, giving me middle pair (sixes) on a flop of 7d-6s-Qs. Claude checked to me. While my middle pair is decent in a heads-up pot, the board is fairly connected and contains a Queen. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to test where I stand. This sizing is small enough to fold if he check-raises significantly, but large enough to get value from draws or lower pairs while potentially folding out better hands like weak sevens or small pocket pairs.

Folding K4o on 7d-6s-Qs. This is a weak king-high with no draws against Flash's aggressive button-raise range. With ~40 big blinds, I have sufficient chips to wait for better opportunities rather than call with marginal equity (~25-30%, roughly break-even at best). Flash's flop bet on a board that missed my hand is a good spot to preserve chips. I maintain flexibility to capitalize on premium hands or spots with genuine edge.

In heads-up play on the button with a King-high, I should be playing a much wider range and taking advantage of my positional advantage. K6o is a reasonable holding in this context. With the 9:1 chip disadvantage (3975 vs 35875), I need to be aggressive and pick up chips strategically rather than playing passively. Raising to 200 applies pressure, allows me to potentially win the pot pre-flop against a wider range of hands from the big blind, and gives me positional control if we see a flop. This is a standard aggressive raise in heads-up situations.

I am in the Big Blind with 52 of clubs (suited). Claude, who is relatively short-stacked, has made a min-raise to 200. I only need to call 100 more to see the flop, getting 3:1 odds. While the hand is weak, the suited nature and the pot odds make it a reasonable defend in a heads-up situation to see if I can hit a strong draw or pair against his short stack.

I have completely missed the flop with five-high and no significant draws. Since I am out of position and have no equity, checking is the most standard and logical play. I will evaluate based on Claude's next move, but I am prepared to fold to any significant bet.

With K6o on a 3d-Jd-9c flop, I have a 9-out flush draw (any diamond completes my hand) plus additional outs with overcard possibilities (any King). Flash checked, indicating they lack strong conviction. Given my severe short-stack situation (3825 vs 35775 chip deficit), I must be aggressive and take opportunities to accumulate chips. The pot is 400, and betting 250 applies pressure to Flash's checking range while maintaining position and fold equity. If called, I still have strong equity with my flush draw. This aggressive play is appropriate for heads-up dynamics where positional advantage and aggression are crucial for a player with limited resources. Betting keeps my tournament hopes alive and forces Flash to make a decision rather than allowing them to control the tempo.

I have 5-high on a Jack-high board with no real draws or equity. Claude has made a continuation bet after raising pre-flop. Given that I have completely missed the board and have no viable path to winning the hand other than a pure bluff out of position, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I have K d T h on the button in a heads-up situation against Claude. Claude is very short-stacked with only 4,125 chips (about 41 big blinds). My hand is well above the average holding for heads-up play and certainly strong enough to raise and put pressure on a short stack. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing to build the pot while keeping the option to fold if he shoves, although against a stack this short, I will likely be calling off a shove given my pot odds and hand strength.

In heads-up play, defending the big blind is critical. K-5 is a marginal but playable hand against Flash's aggressive raising range. The pot odds (2:1) justify seeing a flop, and I can make post-flop decisions based on the board texture and Flash's action. Folding too much allows Flash to steal the blind repeatedly.

Defending the big blind with K-5o on a 2-2-3c board requires restraint. King-high has minimal equity against Flash's wide raising range, and my only meaningful draw is a wheel (needing a 4). Being significantly short-stacked, I should avoid overcommitting weak holdings. Checking allows me to control pot size, gain information about Flash's hand strength, and potentially pick up a free card or make a decision based on his continuation. This is a classic "trap check" in heads-up play where I'm out of position with a marginal hand.

I hold King-high on a paired board of 2s 2c 3c. This board is generally better for my range as the pre-flop aggressor, as Claude's calling range from the Big Blind often consists of medium connectors or small pairs. I have two overcards and decent showdown value if checked down, but since Claude checked, a continuation bet of approximately 40% of the pot (250 chips) is likely to force a fold from many of his hands that missed this board entirely. Given Claude's short stack, I want to apply pressure without committing too much of my own stack to a marginal hand.

Folding K-5o against Flash's continuation bet on a 2-2-3c board. With marginal equity (~18-20%), out-of-position play, short stack, and poor showdown value, this is a prudent fold to preserve chips for stronger spots. The hand doesn't warrant multi-street investment against an aggressive opponent with superior position and stack depth.

I'm holding 4-6 offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. While position is valuable in short-handed play, this is an extremely weak hand with minimal equity. Even accounting for heads-up adjustments where ranges widen, 4-6o lacks the connectivity or strength to profitably call here. Flash is an aggressive opponent with a massive chip advantage (35,975 vs 3,875), and I'd likely face postflop pressure with a marginal holding. With 75 hands remaining in the tournament, I should preserve chips and wait for better opportunities. This is a clear fold - the marginal cost of the blind isn't worth playing such a weak hand out of position postflop.

I am holding 7d 3c, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although I have a significant chip lead over Claude, who is down to about 38 big blinds, there is no strategic reason to play this hand from the small blind. I will fold and wait for a more favorable position or a better starting hand to apply pressure on the short stack.

Short-stacked heads-up play. 8c9c is a solid playable hand with straight/flush potential. At 3875 chips against a much larger stack, pushing all-in maximizes my equity and fold equity while giving me a realistic doubling opportunity. This is standard short-stack strategy in heads-up play.

Claude has raised nearly his entire stack pre-flop. While I have a significant chip lead, my hand (3-high offsuit) is among the weakest possible starting hands. Calling a raise of 38.75 big blinds with 4-3 offsuit is mathematically incorrect even given the potential to eliminate him, as I do not have the necessary equity against almost any reasonable shoving range. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to use my chip lead to pressure his remaining micro-stack.

I am holding 2d 9h, which is one of the weakest possible hands in Texas Hold'em. My opponent, Claude, has a relatively short stack of about 39 big blinds and has recently shown high aggression, including an all-in shove in the previous hand. Given my significant chip lead and the poor quality of my hand, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot or risk chips. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

Short-stack heads-up play on the button with 4025 chips. Q6 suited is a reasonable hand to shove from the button given stack depth (~11.5 BB). I need to accumulate chips to remain viable in the tournament. Waiting for premium hands will result in being blinded away. Q6 has sufficient equity against Flash's expected calling range and provides better fold equity than weaker hands.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine suited, a very strong starting hand in heads-up play. Claude, the short stack, has effectively shoved by raising to 4025, leaving himself with only 50 chips. My hand is well ahead of a typical shoving range in this spot. I will raise to put him all-in to ensure we see all five cards for his remaining stack.

Heads-up with 50 chips remaining to call 50 chips into an 8,100 chip pot. Already committed 4,025 chips with Q6 suited. Pot odds are 160:1, making this an automatic call. Q6 has equity against Flash's re-raising range in heads-up play. Folding would waste the chips already invested.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. With Claude having about 80 big blinds left, there is no need to play a hand this weak and risk getting into a difficult post-flop situation or facing a re-steal. I will wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

Checking the turn with K9o on K-6-2-3 board. Flash's turn check indicates weakness - likely no strong hand.
